verbatim: [§174] I suppose that you are not unaware of the fact that the government of the state is handed on by the older men to the youth of the coming generation; and that since the succession goes on without end, it follows of necessity that as is the education of our youth so from generation to generation will be the fortune of the state.
reasoning: This describes the positive side of the succession rule: the youth are the recognized recipients of the state's governing power as they mature.
rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates, an Athenian, describes the intergenerational transfer of political power within the Athenian state, specifically mentioning the youth.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule is contemporary to Isocrates, who wrote 'Antidosis' around 353 BCE.
author: Isocrates
description: Greek rhetorician and writer (436–338 BC)

verbatim: [§65] For it is not safe for them to participate in the highest offices (for injustice and folly would inevitably cause them to act unjustly in some things and to make mistakes in others)... For this reason Solon and certain other lawgivers appoint the common citizens to the election of the magistrates and the function of calling them to audit, although they do not allow them to hold office singly.
reasoning: Aristotle notes that common citizens (defined as those not rich) are explicitly barred from holding individual high offices, though they may participate in elections and audits.
rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle explicitly references Solon, a key figure in Athenian law, and discusses Athenian political structures.
rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle (floruit -348) discusses contemporary practices while explicitly referencing Solon's laws from Archaic Athens (-594).

resource: Access to landscape and art therapy
verbatim: [§16] The delight of the sight is to be studied as to plants, painting, waters, so that everything may be regarded with pleasure. ... The prospect should be towards meadows, fountains, and babbling streams, for the sweet exhalations from them, and the delightful view, warm the soul and refresh nature. ... But if from want one is not fortunate enough to possess these things, we must make an imitation of the cool breeze, by fanning with the branches of fragrant boughs.
reasoning: The author distinguishes between those who possess the wealth/property to have access to specific healing environments (meadows, fountains, paintings) and those who suffer from 'want' (poverty), who must rely on cheaper imitations (fanning with boughs).
rule_polity_reasoning: Aretaeus of Cappadocia was a Greek physician writing within the Roman Empire, and the medical practices described reflect his contemporary society.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule describes medical practices contemporary to Aretaeus, whose floruit is dated to 135 CE.

verbatim: [§27] ...similarly, in women, the complete disappearance of the monthly discharge,or an undue evacuation such as is caused by violent bleeding from the womb, often provoke dropsy; and in some of them the so-called female flux ends in this disorder.
reasoning: The text identifies specific physiological conditions unique to the female group (menstrual issues) that result in a loss of protection from the disease of dropsy.

resource: Access to public libations and worship
verbatim: [§275] ...even a descendant of Harmodius and of the greatest of all your benefactors, the men to whom, in requital of their glorious deeds, you have allotted by statute a share of your libations and drink-offerings in every temple and at every public service, whom, in hymns and in worship, you treat as the equals of gods and demigods.
reasoning: Statutory rules grant specific lineage groups (descendants of significant benefactors like Harmodius) the right to participate in or be honored during religious public services.
rule_polity_reasoning: Demosthenes refers to the established religious honors for the descendants of Harmodius, a practice originating in Classical Athens.
rule_time_reasoning: The honors for Harmodius and Aristogeiton were established after the overthrow of the tyranny in -510 BCE, a historical practice by Demosthenes' time.
author: Demosthenes
description: classical Athenian statesman and orator (384–322 BC)

verbatim: [§251] Suppose the case of men who, having inherited large fortunes from their ancestors, used their wealth, not to render themselves serviceable to the state, but to outrage their fellow-citizens [...] would anyone venture to put the blame upon the authors of their wealth instead of demanding that the offenders themselves be punished?
reasoning: The rhetorical question implies a social and potentially legal expectation that those with inherited wealth must use it to serve the state rather than for private outrage, effectively a restriction on the absolute right to retain property.
rule_polity_reasoning: Isocrates, an Athenian, discusses the social expectations and obligations of wealthy citizens towards the state in Athens.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule describes contemporary Athenian social and civic expectations for the wealthy during Isocrates' floruit around 400 BCE.
author: Isocrates
description: Greek rhetorician and writer (436–338 BC)

verbatim: [§113] and the Syracusans after the period of the tyrants conferred citizenship on their foreign troops and mercenaries and then faction set in and they came to battle; and the Amphipolitans having received settlers from Chalcis were most of them driven out by them.
reasoning: The text describes historical instances where foreigners who were granted citizenship were later excluded or driven out due to civil strife, indicating their tenuous access to the resource of citizenship.
rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly references the Syracusans and Amphipolitans, indicating specific historical examples.
rule_time_reasoning: The events in Syracuse refer to the period after the tyrants, likely Dionysius I and II, which is prior to Aristotle's floruit.

verbatim: [§16] But when you come to your hero’s acts of humanity, his pecuniary sacrifices, his grand political achievements... the public entertainments he gave, the public burdens he assumed, the ships, the wall, the trench he contributed to, the prisoners he ransomed...
reasoning: The text lists 'public burdens' and 'pecuniary sacrifices' (liturgies) as standard expectations or rules for prominent citizens in Athens, where they provide material resources (ships, walls, ransoms) for the state.
rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian, writing in the Roman Empire, explicitly describes the public service obligations (liturgies) of citizens in Classical Athens.
rule_time_reasoning: The rule describes the practice of liturgies in Classical Athens, which was in effect from the 5th to 4th centuries BCE, long before Lucian's floruit in 150 CE.
